channel.title: NYT > Arts
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: On America’s 250th, Smithsonian Museums Offer Respite and Reflection
item.description: An institution under attack from the Trump administration provided relief from the weather on July 4 — and a chance to ponder what it means to be American.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor and Lawren Simmons
item.category: National Museum of African American History and Culture
National Museum of the American Latino
item.media:description: At the National Museum of American History on Saturday in Washington, where the high was 101 degrees.

item.title: Klaus Mäkelä Plants His Flag in Opera With Strauss’s ‘Frau’
item.description: “Die Frau Ohne Schatten” is a difficult opera for any conductor. Klaus Mäkelä chose it for his first new production, in front of a global audience.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone

item.title: Ordering Objects at V&A East Storehouse: From a David Bowie Guitar to Beatrix Potter Originals
item.description: Visitors at the V&A East Storehouse can book appointments with artifacts that usually aren’t on show — and can even touch many of them.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall and Max Miechowski
item.media:credit: Max Miechowski for The New York Times

item.title: 7 Inspiring Soccer Documentaries to Stream
item.description: In these nonfiction accounts, the sport is a force for hope in sometimes dire situations.
item.dc:creator: Carlos Aguilar

item.title: Perilous and Preposterous, ‘Cliffhanger’ Brought Stallone Back From the Brink
item.description: Renny Harlin’s high-altitude thriller starring Sylvester Stallone and John Lithgow was peak early 1990s action extravagance.
item.dc:creator: Maya Salam

item.title: Readers Pick the Definitive Films That Capture America
item.description: Though “Idiocracy” and “The Godfather” topped their list, the sheer range of suggestions shows how there are many ways to understand the country.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Goodman

item.title: ‘Human Vapor’ Review: Netflix Remakes a Japanese Sci-Fi Curio
item.description: The director Ishiro Honda took a break from Godzilla with a villain who could turn himself into a cloud of gas. A new series pipes him into the 21st century.
item.dc:creator: Mike Hale
item.media:description: The model Uta Uchida, billed as UTA, stars in “Human Vapor,” based on a 1960s Japanese film. The remake updates the story with anti-authoritarian and anticapitalist themes.

item.title: Catherine Laga’aia, ‘Moana’ Star, on the Funniest Podcast Episode Ever
item.description: “I would just watch it, replay it, watch it, replay it,” the “Moana” star said.
item.dc:creator: Kathryn Shattuck

item.title: Vicki Gunvalson on Her ‘Real Housewives of Orange County’ Return
item.description: When Vicki Gunvalson signed on for “The Real Housewives of Orange County” 20 years ago, no one knew she’d become a quintessential reality TV star.
item.dc:creator: Caryn Ganz and Sela Shiloni
item.category: The Real Housewives of Orange County (TV Program)

item.title: How Pierre Coffin Breathed New Life Into the Minions Franchise With ‘Minions & Monsters’
item.description: The director Pierre Coffin, who also voices the yellow chaos agents, wasn’t interested in returning. Then a producer suggested an idea he couldn’t resist.
item.dc:creator: Nicole Sperling

item.title: A Return to the Familiar at Lincoln Center’s New Dance Festival
item.description: While it was good to see again Rachid Ouramdane’s moving duet from a decade ago, Akram Khan’s new piece was unconvincing.
item.dc:creator: Brian Seibert
item.category: Lincoln Center for the Performing Arts

item.title: A Poet for Everyone, Even Those Who Don’t Read Poetry
item.description: The film “Mary Oliver: Saved by the Beauty of the World” works best when it illuminates her work, whose fans include Stephen Colbert and Oprah Winfrey.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson
item.category: Mary Oliver: Saved by the Beauty of the World (Movie)

item.title: New York Gallery Surrenders 20 Artifacts Thought Looted From Cambodia
item.description: The Madison Avenue business turned over stone statues and other items that it had purchased from an art dealer accused of trafficking in illicit objects.
item.dc:creator: Tom Mashberg and Graham Bowley

item.title: ‘The Five-Star Weekend’ and 6 More Shows to Watch on TV This Week
item.description: The adaptation of Elin Hilderbrand’s book airs and the new iteration of “Little House on the Prairie” begins.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Gonzalez
item.media:description: From left: Regina Hall, Jennifer Garner, D’Arcy Carden and Chloë Sevigny in “The Five Star Weekend.”

item.title: ‘House of the Dragon’ Season 3 Episode 3 Recap: The Rat Banquet
item.description: Rhaenyra’s advent to power starts off rough and doesn’t get much better.
item.dc:creator: Sean T. Collins
item.media:description: It’s not easy being queen: Emma D’Arcy in “House of the Dragon.”

item.title: White House Criticizes Smithsonian Museum for ‘Extreme Political Activism’
item.description: A scathing report released on the Fourth of July says the National Museum of American History downplays the role of the founders while emphasizing social justice.
item.dc:creator: Graham Bowley, Robin Pogrebin and Jennifer Schuessler
item.media:description: A report by the White House’s Domestic Policy Council said, “To the extent that there is a story told at the Smithsonian’s National Museum of American History, it is not one of ‘the victory of freedom and genius of our country’ but one of regret, tragedy and shame.”

item.title: Taylor Swift Wedding: What Her Lyrics Say About Marriage
item.description: While many details of her wedding remain unknown, the songs from her 20-year-career tell us plenty about her thoughts on the institution.
item.dc:creator: Lindsay Zoladz
item.media:credit: Jutharat Pinyodoonyachet for The New York Times
item.media:description: The scene outside Madison Square Garden announcing the marriage of Taylor Swift and Travis Kelce.

item.title: Mike Wallace, Who Wrote an Acclaimed History of New York, Dies at 83
item.description: His 1998 book, “Gotham,” which told the city’s story to 1898, focused on social and economic conflict. It won a Pulitzer Prize and inspired two sequels.
item.dc:creator: Sam Roberts
item.category: Greater Gotham: A History of New York City from 1898 to 1919 (Book)
Gotham: A History of New York City to 1898 (Book)
Gotham at War: A History of New York City from 1933 to 1945 (Book)
item.media:credit: Elizabeth D. Herman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mike Wallace in 2017. The Pulitzer committee said that “Gotham,” which he wrote with Edwin G. Burrows, tied many strands “into a single narrative tapestry that reads like a fast-paced novel.”

item.title: Martin Margiela, One of Fashion’s Most Influential Designers, Auctions Off His Personal Archive
item.description: How Martin Margiela, one of the most influential living designers, came to auction off his personal archive, and what it means.
item.dc:creator: Vanessa Friedman

item.title: Representative Joyce Beatty Still Celebrating Victory in Kennedy Center Legal Battle
item.description: Representative Joyce Beatty, Democrat of Ohio, said her lawsuit challenging the president’s changes to the performing arts center was a fight for justice.
item.dc:creator: Olivia Diaz
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:description: Representative Joyce Beatty said she had been inspired to wage the legal battle out of a desire to protect the civil rights legacy of President John F. Kennedy.

item.title: Why ‘There Will Be Blood’ Is the Most American Movie
item.description: The New York Times Film Critic, Alissa Wilkinson, breaks down how Paul Thomas Anderson’s 2007 epic deconstructs and exemplifies American ideals.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson, Edward Vega and Gabriel Blanco

item.title: Why Loving Good-Bad Movies Feels All Good
item.description: A pop culture reporter explains why she revels in debating movies that cross the line from bad to sublime.
item.dc:creator: Maya Salam

item.title: Photos: See Outside Taylor Swift and Travis Kelce’s Wedding at MSG
item.description: The couple’s private celebration created a public spectacle outside Madison Square Garden.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:credit: Jutharat Pinyodoonyachet for The New York Times

item.title: Five Science Fiction Movies to Stream Now
item.description: This month’s recommendations include a dreamscape space opera, a dark time-travel diner comedy and an existential alien thriller.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli

item.title: 5 New Movies Our Critics Are Talking About This Week
item.description: Whether you’re a casual moviegoer or an avid buff, our reviewers think these films are worth knowing about.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: Bang My Box: The Robin Byrd Story (Movie)

item.title: 4 Funny Specials for a Long Hot Holiday Weekend
item.description: Nikki Glaser, Ryan Hamilton, Ali Siddiq and Ophira Eisenberg take very different approaches to their new hours, but all deliver the goods.
item.dc:creator: Jason Zinoman
item.category: I Used to be Nicer (TV Program)
item.media:description: The comedian Nikki Glaser remains ruthless about getting laughs quickly and often.

item.title: The Most Lavish Celebrity Weddings, From the Royals to Taylor Swift
item.description: We hear the couple has plans this weekend in New York City, as they look for their ever after.
item.dc:creator: Victor Mather
item.media:description: Posh and Becks, engaged.

item.title: John Byrne, an ‘X-Men’ Artist, Returns to Rewrite the Story His Way
item.description: After creative differences led him to step away from the characters that made him famous decades ago, John Byrne calls “Elsewhen” his swan song. He’s not going quietly.
item.dc:creator: Howard Fishman
item.media:description: John Byrne, now 76, illustrated by his fellow comics artist Alex Ross.

item.title: The Best Movies and TV Shows Coming to Netflix in July
item.description: A science-fiction thriller with political overtones and a new version of “Little House on the Prairie” are among this month’s most promising titles.
item.dc:creator: Noel Murray

item.title: Robert Kimball, Who Helped Uncover a Trove of Broadway History, Dies at 86
item.description: He excavated a treasure hoard of manuscripts by George Gershwin, Cole Porter, Richard Rodgers and others that were found in a warehouse in Secaucus, N.J.
item.dc:creator: Barry Singer
item.media:description: Robert Kimball in 1998 at the Cole Porter Foundation in Manhattan. He was an expert on Porter and other giants of American popular song.

item.title: Gary Glitter Is Charged With Assaulting a Young Girl in the 1970s
item.description: British prosecutors have charged the singer with multiple counts of indecent assault of a minor, including one of unlawful sex.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:description: Gary Glitter, whose real name is Paul Gadd, was one of the biggest stars of the glam rock boom of the 1970s, especially thanks to “Rock and Roll (Part 2).”

item.title: Herbert Lust, Collector Who Befriended Giacometti, Dies at 99
item.description: He gravitated toward the work of artists he knew personally, like Alexander Calder and Robert Indiana, in amassing an unusual, important collection.
item.dc:creator: Trip Gabriel
item.media:description: Herbert Lust at his home in Connecticut in 2020, wearing a gold medallion made by the artist Alberto Giacometti, a close friend of his.

item.title: Is There Life Out Here?
item.description: Doug Aitken’s sprawling, elusive video installation asks a lot of questions about contemporary L.A. and its environs, and it’s smart enough not to answer them.
item.dc:creator: Max Lakin

item.title: Fewer Visitors, More Threats: A New Reality for Europe’s Jewish Museums
item.description: Museum directors say they are facing a frustrating misconception: that cultural institutions funded by local taxpayers are somehow associated with Israel.
item.dc:creator: Nina Siegal

item.title: How, and Why, We’re Covering Taylor Swift’s (Maybe) Wedding
item.description: About two dozen reporters and editors from several desks are leading The Times’s coverage of the anticipated festivities in Manhattan this week.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.media:description: “America doesn’t have royal weddings,” said Emma Fitzsimmons, a New York Times reporter who is covering the expected nuptials of Travis Kelce and Taylor Swift. “But this is probably the closest thing.”

item.title: Potent Art Lets ‘We, the People’ Reflect on America’s Past and Present
item.description: The New York Public Library has filled its top floor with a bold account of contemporary artists coming to terms with the state of our nation.
item.dc:creator: Blake Gopnik
item.media:credit: Luis Corzo for The New York Times
item.media:description: Robert Longo’s “Untitled (Falling Flag),” sits behind a contemporary rewriting of the Declaration of Independence by Mindy Beloff.

item.title: ‘Lockbox’ Review: A Demonic Combination
item.description: This silly supernatural thriller about a fiendish shape shifter leaves coherence and clarity behind.
item.dc:creator: Erik Piepenburg

item.title: ‘Young Washington’ Review: Not the Campaigns You Had in Mind
item.description: Before the presidency. Before Yorktown. There was … George, the land surveyor.
item.dc:creator: Ben Kenigsberg

item.title: America in Video Games: The Oregon Trail and Postapocalyptic Vegas
item.description: Other outstanding efforts reimagine the American Revolution, the pioneers’ westward migration and the Great Depression.
item.dc:creator: Harold Goldberg

item.title: In These Thrillers, Vacations Go Very, Very Wrong
item.description: The best-selling author Gillian McAllister recommends novels that blend escapist settings with pulse-pounding intrigue and bloody murder.
item.dc:creator: Gillian McAllister

item.title: In ‘Basura,’ Gloria and Emily Estefan Found a New Rhythm
item.description: The mother and daughter spent years composing the songs and the score for the show. “In every mother-daughter relationship, it’s complicated,” Gloria Estefan said.
item.dc:creator: David Peisner

item.title: Gertrude Abercrombie: Good Witch of the American Absurd
item.description: In Milwaukee, two art exhibitions relish the strangeness of this studious eccentric who saw in inanimate objects an odd force of will.
item.dc:creator: Walker Mimms

item.title: Plans for Unrealized ‘Trump Castle’ Surface 40 Years Later
item.description: The 800-foot-tall residential development was designed by the architects Philip Johnson and John Burgee, but scrapped before construction. An archivist saved the rough draft.
item.dc:creator: Robin Pogrebin
item.media:credit: Luis Corzo for The New York Times
item.media:description: And 40 years ago, a little discussed harbinger of that taste – “Trump Castle.”

item.title: Swifties Delight in Taylor’s Wedding Day
item.description: Fans see, in the pop star, reflections of their own lives: hope, heartbreak and now, finally, happiness.
item.dc:creator: Malia Mendez
item.media:credit: Sarah Pabst for The New York Times
item.media:description: Fan reactions like this one in Buenos Aires were common throughout Taylor Swift’s Eras Tour.

item.title: How Trump Made ‘Y.M.C.A.’ His Anthem, Despite the Village People and Victor Willis’s Mixed Feelings
item.description: On social media, he said the Village People’s lead singer, Victor Willis, was with him “right from the beginning.” But the president has a more complicated history with the group.
item.dc:creator: Aishvarya Kavi
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times
item.media:description: President-elect Donald J. Trump danced to “Y.M.C.A.” along with the Village People during a pre-inauguration event in January last year.

item.title: The Good List: 7 Things to Add Joy to Your Day
item.description: Old menus, vivid lightning and Richard Grant sniffing things.
item.dc:creator: Melissa Kirsch

item.title: How JD Vance Helped bell hooks Back Onto the Best-Seller List
item.description: A grass-roots campaign urged readers to buy copies of the Black feminist writer’s 2002 book “Communion” before Vice President JD Vance’s book of the same name hit shelves.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter
item.category: Communion: Finding My Way Back to Faith (Book)
Hillbilly Elegy: A Memoir of a Family and Culture in Crisis (Book)
Communion: The Female Search for Love (Book)
item.media:credit: David Degner for The New York Times

item.title: Chris Brown’s Former Housekeeper Awarded $13 Million in Dog Mauling
item.description: The housekeeper said in her lawsuit that a dog weighing more than 200 pounds had “savagely” bitten her at the singer’s California home in 2020.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.media:description: The singer Chris Brown at the Grammy Awards in Los Angeles in February.

item.title: A New Era at the Montpellier Dance Festival
item.description: The Montpellier dance festival, with new directors for the first time in more than 40 years, featured the wildly popular (La)Horde.
item.dc:creator: Roslyn Sulcas

item.title: Michael Byrne, British Stage Actor, Dies at 86
item.description: He had a long career in theater, and several small roles in big movies, including “Force 10 From Navarone” and “Indiana Jones and the Last Crusade.”
item.dc:creator: Victor Mather
item.media:description: Michael Byrne in 2013. He began his theater career in the early 1960s, mostly in Britain, including at the Old Vic.

item.title: 8 Essential Songs Produced by Tay Keith
item.description: The Memphis-born producer, who died in June at 29, brought deceptively soulful moodiness to some of the biggest rap hits of the past decade.
item.dc:creator: Brian Josephs
item.media:description: Tay Keith produced four No. 1 hits before his death.

item.title: Kerry Washington, Cat Cohen and Raúl Esparza Star in Off Broadway Shows in July
item.description: Kerry Washington and Kara Young pay tribute to Whoopi Goldberg, while Raúl Esparza takes on Shakespeare in the Park.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli
item.category: Giulia: The Poison Queen of Palermo (Play)
Pepper Slit: Live in Her Living Room (Play)
item.media:credit: Richard Termine for The New York Times
item.media:description: From left: Aubrey Matalon, Maya Sistruck, Jamila Sabares-Klemm, Naomi Serrano, Emily Fink, Bre Jackson and Didi Romero in “Giulia: The Poison Queen of Palermo.”

item.title: The Best Movies and Shows Streaming in July 2026: ‘Elle,’ ‘Silo’ and More
item.description: “Elle” and “Descendants: Wicked Wonderland” arrive, and new “Star Trek: Strange New Worlds” and “Silo” seasons begin.
item.dc:creator: Noel Murray
item.category: Pompeii: Out of Time with Tom Hiddleston (Movie)
Stuart fails to save the universe (TV Program)
Nirvanna the Band the Show the Movie (Movie)
Star Trek: Strange New Worlds (TV Program)

item.title: Canada Will Compete in Eurovision Song Contest
item.description: The country’s national broadcaster confirmed that it is joining the world’s most popular singing contest next year. Some fans are urging it to send a star like Shania Twain.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:description: The Eurovision Song Contest final in Vienna this May.

item.title: Victor Willis, Lead Singer of the Village People, Dies at 74
item.description: Often performing dressed as a helmeted police officer, he also co-wrote the group’s biggest hits, including “Y.M.C.A.” and “Macho Man.”
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall and Isabella Kwai
item.media:description: Victor Willis performing with the Village People in Chicago in 1979. More recently, he became a polarizing figure because of his (and the group’s music’s) association with President Trump.

item.title: Slate’s ‘Culture Gabfest’ Signs Off After 18 Years
item.description: The podcast, which helped spawn the chat-show genre, aired its last episode Wednesday.
item.dc:creator: Reggie Ugwu
item.media:description: From left, Julia Turner, Dana Stevens and Stephen Metcalf, hosts of “Culture Gabfest,” the Slate podcast that ends its 18-year run on Wednesday.

item.title: She Grew Up Loving ‘Legally Blonde.’ Now She’s Elle Woods.
item.description: Lexi Minetree beat out 5,000 other actors to play the teenage heroine of “Elle,” a prequel series. She shares many qualities with her can-do character.
item.dc:creator: Melena Ryzik and Thea Traff
item.media:credit: Thea Traff for The New York Times

item.title: The 10 Most Mind-Altering Theater Moments of 2026 (So Far)
item.description: Our critic chose 10 moments from the theatrical year that shifted her thinking.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.category: What We Did Before Our Moth Days (Play)
item.media:description: Christopher Abbott, center, with Nathan Lane and Laurie Metcalf, in “Death of a Salesman” at the Winter Garden Theater in Manhattan.

item.title: ‘Minions & Monsters’ Review: Third Time’s the Charm
item.description: The latest film of the wildly successful spinoff franchise ushers the Minions into a new era of cultural ubiquity, while bringing some new creative juice.
item.dc:creator: Brandon Yu

item.title: ‘Enola Holmes 3’ Review: A Kidnapping in Malta
item.description: Millie Bobby Brown shines as Sherlock’s younger sister in the playful third movie of this popular franchise.
item.dc:creator: Natalia Winkelman

item.title: America, Onscreen
item.description: Ten writers on the films that get at the hopes, dreams, heartbreak and hilarity of the U.S.A. as it turns 250.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: 30 Fun Things to Do in N.Y.C. in July
item.description: This month brings the nation’s 250th birthday bash, “Les Miserables,” Mark Morris, the World Cup final, Jay-Z at Yankee Stadium and nine nights of Bon Jovi.
item.dc:creator: Erik Piepenburg
item.media:description: The scene near the Brooklyn Bridge at Macy’s 4th of July Fireworks in 2025. The show returns for the nation’s 250th birthday and its own 50th anniversary. If you can’t make it in person, it airs live on NBC and Peacock starting at 8 p.m.
